summary: DHH argues against the false dichotomy of either total apathy or total revolt when it comes to Big Tech dependence. He acknowledges that most people have accepted Big Tech companies are more interested in devouring the world than helping it, but notes that quitting cold turkey is unrealistic for most. Instead, he advocates for incremental reduction in usage, comparing it to gradually cutting back on cigarettes. He points out that these companies walk a precarious tightrope of expectations and even small collective reductions in engagement can have meaningful impact.
key_points: The apathy vs. revolt framing is a false dichotomy that paralyzes people into inaction
Big Tech companies operate on such thin margins of expectation that even small collective reductions matter
Incremental reduction in usage is more sustainable and realistic than going cold turkey
You can mix and match strategies — quit some services entirely while just reducing others
Big Tech companies are experts at helping you not think about their stranglehold on your life
money_quotes: We've almost all come to accept the fact that Big Tech is here less to help the world and more to devour it.
These companies are experts at helping you not think about the structure and stranglehold of their businesses.
You don't have to either resign yourself to your utter insignificance or don a cape while shouting in the wind. There's power in the margins. Tremendous power.

All of these companies are already walking a precarious tightrope of towering expectations. They don't need to miss a quarter by more than a few percent before it's a calamity that'll get everyone's attention.
So here's what you can do: A little bit. It helps. Really.
Every day it'll get a little easier, but you gotta do it. That's the hard part. But it gets easier.
It's not all or nothing. Something counts. Something works.
key_insight: You don't need to completely abandon Big Tech to make a difference — incremental reduction in usage is both more achievable and collectively powerful enough to matter.
